agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an e-commerce or logistics system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment and beyond."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, reversing its downstream effects (inventory reservation, fulfillment queue, etc.)."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated against an order that locks in pricing and creates a receivable. Described by Finance as irreversible."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer; represents a point-of-no-return for physical fulfillment."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The moment an order is created in the system, which Operations states is also the moment invoicing occurs."},{"term":"Invoicing at Placement","definition":"Operations policy of generating an invoice at the instant an order is placed, to lock in pricing."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order may be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from the PM defining the cancellation window. A VP could change this policy (e.g., restrict cancellation to 30 minutes).","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, a cancellation request MUST be accepted."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An invoiced order cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as an irreversible financial event. Cancelling an invoiced order would corrupt the financial ledger — this is a data/state integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has an associated invoice, a cancellation request MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy choice about when to invoice. It could be changed to invoice at shipment or at a different trigger.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice MUST be generated synchronously during the placement process."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed but has not yet shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The order is cancelled successfully (per PM requirement)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has been placed and an invoice has been generated","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The cancellation is rejected because the order has been invoiced (per Finance requirement)"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order is being placed","when":"The placement process completes","then":"An invoice is generated immediately to lock in pricing (per Operations requirement)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"AC-1, AC-2, and AC-3 are all enforced simultaneously","when":"Any order is placed and then a cancellation is attempted before shipment","then":"CONTRADICTION: AC-1 requires acceptance, AC-2 requires rejection (since AC-3 guarantees an invoice exists at placement). All three cannot hold."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed, invoice generated immediately (per ops), customer requests cancellation 1 second later before any shipment activity","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — PM says allow it (pre-shipment), Finance says deny it (already invoiced). This is the core contradiction."},{"scenario":"Invoice generation fails or is delayed during placement","expected_behavior":"If the invoice hasn't been created yet, BR-1 would allow cancellation but BR-3 is violated. The system is in an inconsistent state."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Not addressed by any stakeholder. Unclear whether partial cancellation is permitted."},{"scenario":"Credit note or reversal issued against an invoice","expected_behavior":"Finance says invoicing is irreversible, but standard accounting practice allows credit notes. If credit notes are permitted, cancellation of invoiced orders could be modeled as invoice + credit note rather than invoice deletion."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If Finance's rule wins, customers can never cancel orders, leading to increased chargebacks, refund disputes, and customer service costs. If PM's rule wins, cancelling invoiced orders creates accounting discrepancies unless a credit-note mechanism exists.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing. Any resolution must either decouple invoicing from placement timing, or introduce a reversal mechanism (credit notes) that Finance currently does not acknowledge.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If the Finance constraint makes cancellation impossible, customer satisfaction and retention will suffer significantly.","risk":"Shipping the feature with this contradiction unresolved will either break financial integrity (if cancellations are allowed on invoiced orders) or break user expectations (if cancellations are never allowed). Both outcomes carry serious business risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders at all if Finance+Ops rules dominate, leading to frustration, chargebacks, and churn."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If PM rule dominates and invoiced orders are cancelled without a credit-note mechanism, the general ledger becomes inconsistent."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"May need to change invoicing timing (e.g., invoice at shipment instead of placement) which affects pricing lock-in strategy."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints regardless of which rule wins, unless a clean resolution is found."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Core feature requirement (pre-shipment cancellation) is undeliverable as specified without resolving the invoice constraint."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other in a logical impossibility. BR-1 + BR-2 + BR-3 form an unsatisfiable conjunction: if invoicing happens at placement (BR-3) and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (BR-2), then no order can ever be cancelled, which violates BR-1. The spec quality is poor because no stakeholder acknowledges or resolves this conflict. Confidence is low because we cannot determine correct behavior without stakeholder alignment.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and provable — not a matter of interpretation","Each individual rule is clearly stated and unambiguous in isolation","The domain concepts (order, invoice, shipment, cancellation) are well-understood"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a credit-note mechanism as a workaround","Whether Operations could move invoicing to a later lifecycle stage","Whether there are existing systems or regulatory constraints that lock in the current invoicing timing"],"assumptions":["Invoice generation at placement is synchronous and reliable","There is no existing credit-note or invoice-reversal mechanism in the system","Shipment is a discrete event that occurs strictly after placement"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Logical impossibility across three stakeholder requirements: (1) PM says orders can be cancelled before shipment, (2) Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, (3) Operations says orders are invoiced at placement. Since every order is invoiced the instant it is placed, every order is immediately non-cancellable, making the PM's cancellation window impossible. These three rules form an unsatisfiable set — at least one must be relaxed.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Relax Finance rule — allow cancellation of invoiced orders by introducing a credit-note/reversal mechanism. Invoice stays on the ledger but is offset. This preserves Operations' pricing lock-in and PM's cancellation window.","B: Relax Operations rule — move invoicing to shipment time (or a later trigger) instead of placement. This preserves PM's cancellation window and Finance's invoice-immutability, but Operations loses early pricing lock-in.","C: Relax PM rule — orders cannot be cancelled once placed (since they are immediately invoiced). This preserves Finance and Operations rules but eliminates the cancellation feature entirely.","D: Introduce an intermediate state — create a 'proforma invoice' or 'pricing reservation' at placement that is reversible, and generate the real irreversible invoice only at shipment. This satisfies all three stakeholders with a new domain concept."],"recommendation":"Option D is the strongest resolution. Introduce a 'proforma invoice' or 'price lock' at placement that secures pricing (satisfying Operations) but is explicitly reversible (satisfying PM's cancellation requirement). The real, irreversible invoice is generated at shipment (satisfying Finance). If Option D is too complex, Option A (credit-note mechanism) is the standard accounting practice and should be evaluated next. Stakeholders must convene to choose."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated cannot be implemented together. Any implementation will violate at least one stakeholder's explicit requirement.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 AND BR-2 AND BR-3 are mutually exclusive. Implementing all three creates a system where cancellation is both permitted (BR-1) and prohibited (BR-2 + BR-3) for every order."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an e-commerce or logistics system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of states from placement

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these requirements carefully.

Three statements:
1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

The contradiction is clear:
- If orders are invoiced at placement (ops), and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (finance), then orders can NEVER be cancelled (since they're invoiced immediately).
- But PM says orders can be cancelled any time before shipping.

This is a direct contradiction between explicit stakeholder requirements. All three are stated explicitly, and they cannot all be satisfied simultaneously.


I'm flagging this as a genuine contradiction with low confidence in any single resolution path, since the three constraints are fundamentally incompatible.

These three rules form an unsatisfiable set — at least one must be relaxed.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Relax Finance rule — allow cancellation of invoiced orders by introducing a credit-note/reversal mechanism.
